g.desc: Piper Logo, Title, and Headers
g.title: Piper Logo, Title, and Headers

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Base plate for the Rover robot. The outline shows a long, symmetrical plywood panel with a complex profile. The left side is a wide rectangle with two deep, vertical rectangular notches cut into both the top and bottom edges, creating a "comb" or "ribbed" appearance. Moving toward the right, the piece tapers into a narrower, rounded rectangular "tail." Inside the perimeter, several technical cutouts are visible: a large circle in the center-left, a square flanked by two small holes on the far-left edge, and a cluster of four small mounting holes in the center. The "Piper" logo and website URL are etched vertically in the middle. In the top right corner, the text reads: "Base x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This is the largest flat component in the kit and serves as the main chassis. These details help a student identify the front from the back and ensure it is placed correctly on the component sheet:
The "Comb" End (Rear): One end of the board features four deep, open-ended rectangular slots (two on top, two on bottom). These slots feel like "fingers" or teeth. This section is the widest part of the base.
The "Tail" End (Front): The opposite end is much narrower with a smooth, rounded tip. It contains two small circular holes placed vertically near the very edge.
Central Circular Cutout: There is a large, thumb-sized hole located roughly in the middle of the board. This is a primary landmark for centering the piece.
Edge Texture: As a laser-cut plywood piece, the top and bottom surfaces are smooth with a slight wood grain, while the dark, burnt edges will feel slightly textured or gritty to the touch.
Alignment: When placing this on the blueprint, the "Comb" end should face left, and the narrow "Tail" should point to the right.
g.title: Wood Piece - Base (1)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Motor Module printed circuit board assembly (PCBA). The outline is a simple vertical rectangle with four rounded corners. Unlike the plywood pieces, this component is a thin, rigid fiberglass board populated with various electronic elements. At the bottom of the graphic, the text reads: "Motor Module x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This module is a small, dense electronic "brain" for the motors. Since the board is symmetrical in shape, use these tactile features to orient it correctly on the component sheet:
The Grove Connector (Top): One end of the board features a raised, boxy plastic housing with a notched opening. This is the Grove connector, which feels like a small "mouth" or a hollow plastic cube on the surface of the board.
The Header Pins (Bottom): The opposite end has a row of sharp, thin metallic spikes (male header pins) pointing outward or upward. These are easily felt and distinct from the smooth plastic connector at the other end.
Component Density: One side of the board (the top) will feel "bumpy" due to the various surface-mount resistors, chips, and the large connector. The back side of the board is typically much smoother, though you may feel the small solder points where the pins and connectors are attached.
Material Contrast: The board itself is made of rigid, smooth FR4 (fiberglass), which feels colder and harder than the laser-cut plywood chassis pieces.
Alignment: Place the module so the plastic Grove connector is at the top and the metallic header pins are at the bottom to match the orientation on the blueprint.
g.title: Electronics Component - Motor Module (1)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Range Finder Holder laser-cut plywood piece. The outline is highly irregular and complex, resembling a wide "U" or "cradle" shape. The top edge features two deep, smooth semi-circular scoops with a rounded peak in the center, designed to hold the cylindrical sensors of a range finder. The left and right outer edges each have a small T-shaped tab protruding horizontally. The bottom edge is flat but features two small T-shaped tabs pointing downward and a central rectangular extension. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Range Finder Holder x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This piece acts as a bracket. Its unique "goggle-like" top edge and various interlocking tabs are the best ways to identify it:
The "Cradle" Top: The most distinct feature is the top edge, which has two large, smooth circular cutouts. If you run your finger along the top, it will feel like two deep valleys separated by a central hill.
Side Tabs: On the far left and far right sides, there are small "T" shaped tabs. These are interlocking points that feel like small square bumps sticking out from the main body.
Bottom Alignment Tabs: The bottom edge has two more T-shaped tabs and a small central "leg." These are used to slot the holder into the robot's base.
Surface Texture: Like the other wood pieces, the large flat faces are smooth, while the intricate interior curves of the "cradle" will have the slightly rougher, charred texture of laser-cut edges.
Alignment: Place the piece on the blueprint so the two large scoops are facing "up" (away from you) and the flat edge with the three small protrusions is facing "down" (toward you).
g.title: Title: Wood Piece - Range Finder Holder (1)

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint illustration of the Ultrasonic Range Finder module. The drawing shows a rectangular circuit board base with two large, prominent cylindrical sensors protruding forward, resembling a pair of binoculars or "eyes." On the top edge of the board, centered between the two cylinders, is a small, boxy Grove connector housing. The entire component has a distinct 3D profile compared to the flat plywood pieces. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Ultrasonic Range Finder x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This is one of the most recognizable electronic components in the kit due to its unique shape. Use these features to identify and orient it on the component sheet:
The "Eyes" (Front): The two large metal cylinders are the most obvious tactile feature. They are smooth, cold to the touch, and should be facing away from the circuit board toward the user.
The Grove Connector (Top): Located on the top edge of the board, centered between the two cylinders, is a small, raised plastic cube with a notched opening. This is the plug-in point for the sensor cable.
The Mesh Grills: If you touch the circular faces of the two cylinders, you may feel a very fine, textured metal mesh or a thin vibrating plate inside. This is where the sound waves are sent and received.
Rear Pins: The back of the circuit board is mostly flat but will have several small, sharp solder points where the cylinders and the connector are attached to the PCB.
Alignment: Place the component on the blueprint so the two "eyes" are pointing toward you (or upward, depending on the sheet's layout) and the plastic Grove connector is at the top edge. The rectangular board should fit squarely within the illustrated boundary.
g.title: Electronic Component - Ultrasonic Range Finder (1)

g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of a #4-40 Screw. The graphic shows a side view of a small machine screw with a rounded "pan head" on the left and a threaded cylindrical shaft extending to the right. A measurement line below the screw indicates a length of 1/2". Inside the main boundary, there is a small rounded square icon containing the letter "A," which serves as a part identifier. Below the graphic, the text reads: "#4-40 Screw x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These screws are small, heavy for their size, and made of smooth metal. Use these features to distinguish them from other hardware in the kit:
The Rounded Head: One end of the screw is a wide, smooth, dome-shaped disk. If you press your finger against the top of the disk, you will feel a deep "X" or cross-shaped indentation (the Phillips drive) where the screwdriver fits.
The Threaded Shaft: The body of the screw is a thin cylinder with a distinct "ribbed" or "spiraled" texture. Running a fingernail along the side of the shaft will produce a clicking or vibrating sensation as you pass over the individual threads.
Length Comparison: At 1/2 inch long, this screw is roughly the width of a standard adult fingernail. If you have other screws in the kit, this measurement is key to ensuring you have the correct "A" part.
Material Weight: Unlike the plywood or plastic components, these brass screws feel cold to the touch and significantly denser/heavier for their small size.
Alignment: Place two screws horizontally within the blueprint box. The rounded heads should face left, and the threaded tips should point right to match the illustration.
g.title: Brass Hardware - #4-40 Screw (2)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Motor Holder laser-cut plywood piece. The outline is a simple, horizontal rectangle with four rounded corners. Centered within the rectangle are two small circular cutouts, arranged vertically one above the other. The overall shape is clean and symmetrical. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Motor Holder x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This is one of the smaller structural plywood pieces. Its simplicity makes it easy to identify, but its orientation is important for assembly:
The Rounded Rectangle: The piece is a flat, smooth wooden block. The rounded corners give it a soft feel compared to the sharp edges of the technical components.
Dual Mounting Holes: In the very center of the piece, there are two small holes. You can feel these by running a fingertip across the middle of the flat surface. They are spaced close together and aligned in a straight line.
Symmetry: Unlike the Range Finder Holder, this piece is nearly identical regardless of which way you flip it horizontally. However, it is designed to be placed with the two holes aligned vertically (up and down).
Burned Edges: The perimeter of the rectangle will have the characteristic dark, slightly textured edge from the laser-cutting process, providing a high-contrast feel against the smooth top and bottom faces.
Alignment: Place the piece on the blueprint so that the long sides of the rectangle are horizontal and the two internal holes are stacked vertically in the center.
g.title: Wood Piece - Motor Holder (1)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of Spacer #1, a small laser-cut plywood component. The outline is a compact rectangle with four rounded corners. Inside the rectangle, there are two small circular cutouts positioned horizontally, one on the left and one on the right, mirroring each other. The piece is simple and symmetrical. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Spacer #1 x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This is one of the smallest wooden structural pieces in the kit. Because it is small, its tactile features are the best way to distinguish it from other similar spacers:
Compact Shape: This piece is a small, flat wooden tile. It is noticeably shorter and more "squat" than the Motor Holder.
Dual Horizontal Holes: There are two small holes located near the left and right edges. If you pinch the piece between your thumb and forefinger, you can feel these two openings sitting side-by-side.
Surface & Edge Contrast: The large flat faces of the spacer are smooth wood grain. The outer perimeter and the inside of the two holes will feel slightly rougher and charred due to the laser-cutting process.
Symmetry: This piece is functionally identical regardless of which side is facing up. It can be rotated 180 degrees and still feel the same.
Alignment: Place two of these pieces on the blueprint so the long edges are horizontal and the two internal holes are side-by-side.
g.title: Wood Piece - Spacer #1 (2)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of Spacer #2, a small plastic hardware component. The outline features a large central circle with two smaller circular "ears" or tabs protruding horizontally from the left and right sides. Each of these side tabs contains a small circular mounting hole. The overall shape resembles a simplified head with two large ears or a flat flange bracket. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Spacer #2 x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks Unlike the wooden spacers, these are made of molded plastic, giving them a very different feel and weight. Use these features to identify them:
The "Ear" Shape: The most distinct tactile feature is the wide circular center and the two smaller bumps on the sides. It feels like a small, flat disc with two wings.
Material Contrast: These pieces are made of smooth, hard plastic. They will feel slicker and less "organic" than the plywood spacers, and they lack the charred, gritty edge texture of the laser-cut wood.
Dual Mounting Holes: There is a small hole in each of the side "ears." These holes are smaller than the large central opening and are designed for screws to pass through.
Uniformity: The plastic is molded, so the top and bottom surfaces will feel identical, and the edges will be smooth and consistent all the way around the perimeter.
Alignment: Place two of these spacers on the blueprint so the two small "ears" are oriented horizontally (left and right), matching the orientation of the illustration.
g.title: Plastic Piece - Spacer #2 (2)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Motor Spacer laser-cut plywood piece. The outline is a wide, horizontal "H" or dumbbell-like shape. It consists of two large, rounded square ends connected by a narrow central bridge. Each of the square ends features a deep, thin vertical slot cut upward from the bottom edge, resembling a two-pronged fork or a "clamping" mechanism. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Motor Spacer x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This piece is highly structural and has a very specific "bridge and slot" design that makes it stand out from the other spacers:
The Central Bridge: This is a long, narrow strip of wood that connects the two larger ends. It serves as a handle or spacer bar and feels thin compared to the bulky ends.
The Slotted "Forks": Each end of the piece has a deep, narrow notch cut into it. If you slide a fingernail into these openings, they will feel like thin, parallel "fingers" of wood. These slots are designed to slide over other components.
Rounded Square Ends: The outer corners of the large ends are smooth and rounded, contrasting with the straight, sharp interior lines of the vertical slots.
Edge Texture: The perimeter of this complex shape, especially inside the narrow slots, will have the dark, slightly gritty texture of laser-cut wood grain.
Alignment: Place two of these spacers on the blueprint so the connecting bridge is horizontal. Ensure the two open slots are facing "down" (toward the bottom of the sheet) as shown in the illustration.
g.title: Wood Piece - Motor Spacer (2)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of an O-Ring. The graphic consists of a single, perfect black circle with a thin stroke. There are no internal features or cutouts, representing a simple, continuous loop. Below the graphic, the text reads: "O-Ring x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This is a small, flexible hardware component. Its material and shape make it distinct from the rigid wood and plastic parts in the kit:
The Circular Loop: The O-Ring is a continuous, unbroken circle. It feels like a small, thick loop of string or a tiny rubber band that has a consistent, round cross-section rather than being flat.
Material Flexibility: Unlike the plywood or plastic spacers, this piece is squishy and elastic. You can deform its shape by squeezing it between your fingers, and it will snap back into a circle once released.
Surface Texture: The rubber is smooth and may feel slightly "grippy" or tacky to the touch. It lacks the grain of the wood or the slick, hard finish of the plastic spacers.
Weight: This is one of the lightest components in the kit. It has very little mass compared to the brass screws or the electronic modules.
Alignment: Because it is a perfect circle, the O-Ring has no specific horizontal or vertical orientation. Simply place it flat within the circular boundary on the blueprint.
g.title: Rubber Hardware - O-Ring (1)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Velcro Pieces. The graphic shows two small, overlapping squares. One square is positioned slightly behind and to the left of the other, which is rotated at a slight angle. Above the squares, an exclamation point icon inside a circle is centered, followed by the warning text: "Do not remove once attached." At the bottom of the graphic, the text reads: "Velcro Pieces x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These are soft, adhesive-backed squares that feel very different from the wood and plastic parts. Since they come as a mated pair, use these features to identify them:
The Two Textures: If you separate the pieces, one side (the "hook") will feel scratchy, rough, and prickly. The other side (the "loop") will feel soft, fuzzy, and fabric-like.
The Adhesive Backing: The reverse side of both squares is covered by a smooth, slick waxy paper or plastic film. This film must be peeled off to reveal a sticky surface.
Flexible Square Shape: These are small, thin, and flexible. They do not have the rigidity of the plywood or the weight of the brass screws.
The "Stick": When the two squares are pressed together, they interlock. You can feel a distinct resistance and hear a "crackle" sound when pulling them apart.
Alignment: Place the set of Velcro squares within the boundary on the blueprint. Because they are squares, their specific rotation isn't critical for the component sheet, but they should be kept together until you are ready to attach them to the robot.
g.title: Fastener - Velcro Pieces (1)
Brass Hardware - #2-56 Screw (2)
g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of a #2-56 Screw. The graphic shows a side view of a very long, thin machine screw. On the left is a small, rounded "pan head," and extending to the right is a long, slender threaded shaft. A measurement line below the screw indicates a length of 1". Inside the main boundary, there is a small rounded square icon containing the letter "B," which serves as a part identifier. Below the graphic, the text reads: "#2-56 Screw x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This screw is notably thinner and longer than the other hardware in the kit. Use these tactile features to identify it and distinguish it from the "A" screws:
The Long, Thin Shaft: This is the primary landmark. At 1 inch long, it is twice the length of the "A" screw. It feels significantly more delicate and "needle-like" because it is much thinner.
The Tiny Rounded Head: One end of the screw is a small, smooth, dome-shaped disk. Despite its small size, you can still feel the "X" or cross-shaped Phillips drive indentation on the top surface.
Fine Threads: The body of the screw has very fine, closely spaced threads. Running a fingernail along the shaft will produce a very fast, light vibration compared to the coarser threads of larger screws.
Material & Weight: Like the other screws, this is made of brass. It feels cold and metallic, though because it is so thin, it feels much lighter and more flexible than the "A" screw.
Alignment: Place two of these screws horizontally within the blueprint box. The rounded heads should face left, and the long threaded tips should point to the right to match the illustration.

A blueprint illustration of a 4-Pin Cable. The graphic shows a bundle of four thin, flexible wires looping in a "U" shape. On the top right end of the loop is a wide, rectangular plastic housing (the Grove connector). On the bottom right end, the four individual wires terminate in separate, narrow cylindrical tips (the male pins). Below the graphic, the text reads: "4-Pin Cable x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This cable serves as the "nervous system" connecting your modules. It has two very different ends that are easy to distinguish by touch:
The Grove Connector (One End): This is a single, large plastic block. It feels like a small, flat rectangle with a notched "key" on one side. This end is designed to click into the boxy connectors on your Motor Module or Range Finder.
The Male Pins (Other End): Instead of a single block, this end has four distinct, thin metallic spikes. Each pin is encased in a small, hard plastic sleeve. You can feel these as four separate "fingers" at the end of the wire bundle.
The Wire Ribbon: The middle of the cable consists of four thin wires bonded together side-by-side. It feels like a flat, flexible ribbon. You can feel the individual ridges of the four wires if you run your thumbnail across the width of the ribbon.
Flexibility: Unlike the rigid plywood or PCBAs, this part is limp and can be coiled or bent easily.
Alignment: When placing these on the component sheet, lay them so the large plastic Grove connector is at the top right and the four individual metal pins are at the bottom right, following the curve shown in the illustration.
g.title: Cable - 4-Pin Grove to Male Header (2)

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint illustration of the Ball and Holder component, which acts as the front caster wheel for the robot. The drawing shows a 3D assembly consisting of a circular plastic housing with two small, flat "ears" or mounting tabs extending from the sides. A smooth, hemispherical ball protrudes from the bottom of the housing. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Ball and Holder x 1."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This component is unique as it is a mechanical assembly with a moving part. Use these tactile features to identify it and place it on the component sheet:
The Rolling Ball (Bottom): The most distinct feature is the hard, smooth sphere captured inside the housing. It should spin freely when you move your finger across it. This is the "wheel" that touches the ground.
Mounting "Ears" (Top): Opposite the ball, the top of the housing is flat with two small rectangular tabs sticking out. Each tab has a circular hole for a screw. These feel like small, flat wings.
Cylindrical Body: The main body of the holder is a thick plastic cylinder. It feels much sturdier and heavier than the plastic spacers.
Material: Like the other plastic parts, this has a slick, non-porous finish. It does not have the wood grain or charred edges found on the plywood pieces.
Orientation: Place the component on the blueprint so the two mounting ears are aligned horizontally (left and right) and the rolling ball is facing "down" (or toward you) as depicted in the 3D illustration.
g.title: Title: Plastic Piece - Ball and Holder (1)

g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of a #4-40 Nut shown from an isometric perspective. The graphic depicts a small, hexagonal (six-sided) metal ring with a threaded circular hole in the center. The top and bottom surfaces are flat, while the six vertical sides create a faceted outer perimeter. Below the graphic, the text reads: "#4-40 Nut x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This nut is designed to pair with the #4-40 "A" screws. It is very small and requires fine motor skills to identify and orient:
The Hexagonal Perimeter: The most distinct feature is its six-sided shape. If you roll it between your thumb and forefinger, you will feel six distinct flat "faces" and six crisp corners.
The Threaded Center: The hole in the middle is not smooth; it contains internal spiral grooves (threads) that match the #4-40 screw. You can feel the hollow center by pressing a fingertip against the flat face.
Flat Parallel Faces: The top and bottom of the nut are perfectly flat and smooth. This is the part that will sit flush against the plywood or plastic spacers during assembly.
Material & Weight: Made of brass, it feels cold, hard, and metallic. Despite its tiny size, it has a noticeable "clink" if dropped on a hard surface.
Scale: This nut is quite small—roughly the width of a pencil eraser. It is much thinner than it is wide.
Alignment: Place two of these nuts within the blueprint box. They are symmetrical, so as long as one of the flat faces is resting against the sheet, they are correctly oriented.
g.title: Brass Hardware - #4-40 Nut (2)

g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of a #4-40 Screw, identified as part C. The graphic displays a side profile of a long machine screw. It features a rounded "pan head" on the far left, followed by a long cylindrical shaft that is fully threaded to the right. A measurement line positioned below the shaft indicates a total length of 1". Centered within the boundary is a small rounded square icon containing the letter "C." Below the graphic, the text reads: "#4-40 Screw x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks This screw is a longer version of the "A" screw. Identifying it correctly is essential for structural stability in specific parts of the Rover:
The Rounded Head: The left end of the screw is a smooth, dome-shaped disk. You can feel the Phillips "X" indentation on the top where the screwdriver seats.
The Long Threaded Shaft: At 1 inch in length, this screw is twice as long as the "A" screw and shares the same thickness. The entire length of the shaft has a "ribbed" or spiraled texture that can be felt with a fingernail.
Part Identification (C): While physically similar in diameter to the "A" screw, its length is the distinguishing factor. Use a ruler or the measurement guide on the blueprint to confirm it matches the 1" requirement.
Material & Density: Made of brass, this hardware feels noticeably heavier than the plastic or wood components and will feel cold to the touch initially.
Alignment: Place two of these screws horizontally in the designated zone. The heads should be on the left, and the threaded tips should point toward the right.
g.title: Brass Hardware - #4-40 Screw (2)

g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of a Small Screw, identified as part D. The graphic shows a side profile of a very short machine screw. It features a rounded "pan head" on the top left and a short, stubby threaded shaft that points diagonally down toward the right. A small rounded square icon containing the letter "D" is centered within the boundary as a part identifier. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Small Screw x 2".
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These are the shortest screws in the kit and are used for making delicate connections where a longer shaft would be obstructive:
Stubby Profile: The most defining tactile feature is its length. The threaded shaft is extremely short, making the entire piece feel like a small metal stud rather than a traditional long screw.
The Head: Like the larger brass screws, the top has a smooth, rounded dome. You can feel the Phillips "X" indentation on the top where the screwdriver seats.
Pointed Tip: Unlike the flat-bottomed machine screws, the tip of this screw is sharp and pointed. You can feel this sharp point by carefully pressing your finger against the end of the threaded shaft.
Material & Weight: Made of brass, the screw feels cold, hard, and metallic. Because it is so small, it is very light and can be easily dropped or lost; handle with care.
Alignment: Place two of these screws within the blueprint box. On the sheet, they are illustrated at a 45-degree angle with the heads pointing toward the upper-left corner.
g.title: Metal Hardware - Small Screw (2)

g.desc:
A 3D isometric blueprint illustration of the FM90 Geared Motor. The component is shown as a boxy, rectangular motor housing. On the front face, there is a circular output shaft assembly with a smaller raised cylinder in the center. The housing features a thin mounting flange with a small vertical notch on the side for securing it to a bracket. Below the graphic, the text reads: "FM90 Geared Motor x 2".
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These motors provide the power for your robot's movement. They are heavy and complex, with several distinct tactile zones:
The Main Body: A large, solid rectangular block made of hard plastic. It feels smooth on all sides and is one of the heaviest individual components in the kit.
The Output Shaft (Front): On one of the smaller rectangular faces, you will find a circular assembly. In the very center is a small, raised plastic peg or "D-shaft" that rotates. This is where the wheels will eventually attach.
Mounting Flanges: The front face of the motor is slightly wider than the main body, creating "wings" or flanges. Each wing has a small U-shaped notch cut into it. These notches are designed to slide into the slots of the Wood Motor Spacers or align with the Motor Holder.
Wiring Connection (Rear/Side): On the side or back of the motor, you may feel small metallic solder tabs or wires protruding. These are the electrical connection points and are much more delicate than the rest of the housing.
Alignment: Place the two motors within the designated zones on the blueprint. Orient them so the circular output shafts are facing "forward" and to the right, matching the perspective in the illustration.
g.title: Electronic Hardware - FM90 Geared Motor (2)

g.desc:
A blueprint illustration of the Wheel, which acts as the rubber tire for the robot. The graphic depicts two concentric circles, creating a thick ring-like appearance. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Wheel x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These wheels are the primary points of contact between your robot and the ground. They are significantly larger and thicker than the O-Ring:
The Tire "Tread": This is a thick, hollow ring made of soft, grippy rubber. Unlike the O-Ring, which has a round cross-section, the tire is wider and flatter on the outside "tread" surface.
Hollow Center: The middle of the tire is empty, designed to be stretched over a plastic wheel hub. If you stick a finger through the center, you will feel the inner rim of the rubber.
Inner Groove: If you feel the inside surface of the rubber ring, you may notice a small lip or groove designed to lock the rubber onto the plastic wheel so it doesn't slip off during movement.
High Friction: The material is designed to be "sticky" to provide traction. It will feel much more substantial and heavier than the O-Ring.
Alignment: Since it is a symmetrical ring, there is no specific orientation for the component sheet. Place both rubber tires flat within the concentric circle boundaries on the blueprint.
g.title: Rubber Hardware - Wheel (2)

g.desc:
A technical blueprint illustration of the Wheel Rim. The graphic shows a circular plastic hub designed with a "five-spoke" pattern. In the very center is a solid circular hub containing three small holes arranged in a horizontal line. Five support spokes extend outward from this center to meet a thin outer circular rim. Below the graphic, the text reads: "Wheel Rim x 2."
Tactile & Orientation Landmarks These rims serve as the skeleton for the robot’s main drive wheels. They are rigid and have several distinct geometric features:
The Outer Rim: A thin, smooth circular edge that runs around the entire perimeter. This is where the rubber tire will be mounted.
The Five Spokes: Between the center and the outer edge, you can feel five distinct gaps or "windows." The spokes themselves are thin, flat strips of plastic.
Central Mounting Hub: The middle of the wheel is a solid, flat disc. This is the sturdiest part of the component.
The Three Center Holes: In the very center of the hub, there are three tiny holes in a row. The middle hole is designed to press-fit onto the motor's output shaft, while the two outer holes can be used for securing the rim with screws if necessary.
Material: Made of hard, lightweight plastic. It feels very smooth and slick compared to the wooden components, and it lacks the weight of the brass hardware.
Alignment: Place two of these rims within the circular boundaries on the blueprint. Since they are circular, their rotation doesn't matter for the component sheet, but ensure the side with the three central holes is facing upward.
g.title: Plastic Hardware - Wheel Rim (2)
